card.intuition: For DP recurrences of the form dp[i] = min over j < i of (m[j] * x[i] + b[j]), each candidate j defines a line y = m[j]*x + b[j]. The minimum at query x[i] is the lower envelope of all lines — maintained as a convex hull. Adding/querying takes amortised O(1) when queries are monotone.
card.analogy: Finding the cheapest supplier as your order size grows: each supplier quotes a different base price and per-unit cost. For any given order size, the cheapest is on the 'lower convex hull' of all supplier cost lines — and as order size increases, the optimal supplier only changes in one direction.
card.time_complexity: O(n) when lines and queries are both monotone; O(n log n) with Li Chao tree for arbitrary order
when_to_use: DP recurrences dp[i] = min(m[j]*x[i] + b[j]) over j — linear function minimisation
Whenever a naive O(n²) DP has cost function decomposable into lines
gotchas: Monotone CHT only works when lines are added in order of slope AND queries are monotone
For general order, use a Li Chao tree (O(n log C) per operation)
The 'bad line' condition: line l2 is made obsolete by l1 and l3 if their intersection x(l1,l3) ≤ x(l1,l2)

exercise.prompt: Implement monotone CHT (deque-based) for DP with lines in decreasing slope order and increasing queries.
hints: After popping dominated lines, the best line is at the front of the deque: self.hull[0]
exercise.test_code:
# dp[i] = min over j<i of (dp[j] + cost), with lines having slope=2*j, intercept=dp[j]+j^2
# Simple test: lines y=2x+0, y=x+3, y=0x+10
cht = MonotoneCHT()
cht.add_line(2, 0)   # y = 2x
cht.add_line(1, 3)   # y = x+3
cht.add_line(0, 10)  # y = 10
assert cht.query(0) == 0   # 2*0=0 wins
assert cht.query(3) == 6   # 2*3=6, 1*3+3=6 (tie), 10 -> 6
assert cht.query(4) == 7   # 2*4=8, 1*4+3=7 wins
assert cht.query(8) == 10  # 2*8=16, 1*8+3=11, 10 wins
print('PASS')
